poems.title: Song of the First Sickle
lines: Reap, reap, reap!
Reap down a stretch of sunset, reap ten thousand waves of wheat;
At last we have lived to see this day,
At last the vast frontier glitters with our golden fields.
The night is not quiet either — all things grow,
The jubilant snowmelt is the pulsing blood of Junggar;
The sound of corn stalks jointing is so crisp and clear,
It startles the mischievous deer and the timid gazelles.
Gather round — the harvest is our flag!
Every ear of grain a bullet
Fired at the desert
That said nothing would ever grow here.

poems.title: Blooming in a Burst
lines: Setting sail on the Gurbantünggüt sea,
The combine harvester launches in the dim dawn light,
Heading into the glinting, golden waves;
The wheat bows before the blade
Like a golden tide retreating,
And the pioneers cheer
As the granaries fill
With the bread of a new world
Blooming in a burst.

poems.title: The Spring Wind Crossed the Jade Gate Long Ago
lines: The spring wind crossed the Jade Gate long ago,
Yet the mighty Tianshan still wears its crystal crown of snow;
Add another shovelful of rich soil for the new saplings,
For green is the color of conquest
On this frontier where only sand once ruled.
The old poem said:
'The spring wind never reaches west of Jade Gate Pass' —
But we have brought the spring wind with us
In our pockets, in our songs
In the seeds we carry
From the warm south
To this cold, bright north.

annotations.note: Yumen Pass (Jade Gate) — a Han Dynasty border pass northwest of Dunhuang, an obligatory choke point on the northern Silk Road. Wang Zhihuan's line 'the spring wind does not cross Yumen Pass' made it immortal.

poems.title: Advancing into the Great Northwest
lines: You have received letters from far away before,
But never from a place this distant;
This letter will cross the breadth of the land,
Past rivers and mountain ranges you have only read about.
We marched here with our bedrolls on our backs,
Soldiers yesterday, farmers today;
The desert did not welcome us —
We welcomed ourselves
With shovels and songs
And the stubborn belief
That even sand can learn to bloom.

annotations.note: The Great Northwest — a general term for northwestern China, including Xinjiang, Gansu, Qinghai, Ningxia, and Shaanxi. Post-1949, vast numbers of youth and soldiers were sent here to develop the region.

poems.title: A Small Poem Written in the Dugout — Chronicles of the Sand Dunes
lines: It will take decades, perhaps half a century,
Before this sand-dune outpost becomes a city;
But tonight in the dugout we write poems by candlelight,
And dream the dreams of builders
Who can see skyscrapers in sand.
'Sand-Dune City' — that's what we call this place,
Two dunes and a handful of soldiers
Living like moles beneath the earth,
Eating army rations, drinking snowmelt,
Writing letters home that say:
'All is well. The future is bright.
You would not believe the stars out here.'

annotations.note: Dugout (dìwōzi) — semi-underground shelters constructed and inhabited by early military settlers in Xinjiang, offering essential protection from fierce sandstorms and extreme temperature variations.

poems.title: By Tianshan Moonlight — To Abdou
lines: I seem to see you standing by the forge again,
Your embroidered cap still reflecting the smoke of the northwestern front;
The sparks flying from your hammer
Light up the night like little stars.
Abdou — Uyghur blacksmith, brother in arms —
You forged our kantuman hoes from scrap iron,
The tools that broke the desert's back,
Shaped by your dark, skilled hands
In the orange heat of the furnace,
By Tianshan moonlight.

annotations.note: Tianshan Mountains — a massive mountain system in Central Asia traversing the entirety of central Xinjiang, stretching about 2,500 kilometers and acting as the region's geographical backbone.

poems.title: O, Red Willow
lines: O, red willow,
Your leaves calmly unfurl against the west wind,
Your branches furiously shatter the storm clouds;
Deepest root in the alkali flats,
Brightest bloom in the salt-white waste,
You taught us how to live here:
Drink the bitter water
And flower anyway.
O, red willow,
Soldier of the desert,
Poet of the sand —
We named our roads after you,
And our daughters too.

annotations.note: Red Willow (Tamarisk) — a remarkably drought-resistant desert shrub with deep roots. In Junggar poetry, it is the foremost symbol of the tenacity and vitality of the frontier settlers.

poems.title: Gleaming with Moonlight
lines: Gleaming with moonlight,
Gleaming with starlight;
Snow waves clustering, singing their lyric night song,
The Tianshan rises like a luminous wall
Between us and the sky.
The night murmurs with growing things,
Cotton bolls splitting open in the dark,
Irrigation water whispering through the channels,
And somewhere, a harmonica
Playing a tune from Shandong,
Here, beneath the stars of Junggar.

poems.title: Lighting a Flickering Campfire — To the Deer
lines: Here,
We light a flickering campfire,
And from this shining moment a new epoch of the desert begins;
The deer watch from the tamarisk thickets,
Their eyes catching the firelight;
They do not fear us yet,
These first pioneers,
Who smell of earth and sweat
Rather than gunpowder.
Dear deer — we are your new neighbors,
The two-legged creatures
Who sing off-key by firelight
And mean you no harm.

poems.title: Dreaming a Green Dream beneath the Dune
lines: Already we have bid farewell to the brocade paddy fields,
Already we have bid farewell to the clustered new villages —
Not our Jiangnan homeland,
But a place that almost felt like home.
Now under the dune we dream a green dream,
A dream of orchards and canals,
Of schools and clinics,
Of children who will know
Only the green of things growing,
Not the brown of the march.

poems.title: Tonight They Cross the Red Willow Bridge Again
lines: We once crossed stone bridges,
We once crossed single-plank bridges;
How many bridges we have crossed in a lifetime,
But this bridge of bundled red willow branches
Is the one we will remember.
It sways beneath our boots,
This bridge that we built ourselves
From the desert's only timber,
The indestructible red willow.
Tonight they cross the red willow bridge again,
Heading to the dam site,
Heading to the future.

poems.title: Drink This Bowl of Tianshan Snowmelt
lines: The old soldiers once welcomed us this way,
Welcoming the new recruits who came to conquer the desert;
Drink this bowl of Tianshan snowmelt,
Clear as crystal, cold as the mountain's heart.
This is our wine, our welcome toast —
Not grape wine, not rice wine,
But the purest water on earth,
Straight from the glacier
To your cracked lips.
Drink — and become one of us.

poems.title: They Call You the 'Wild Goose of the Harvest'
lines: Skimming over the shelter belts, over the striped fields,
The Gurbantünggüt sea sends up clouds and waves;
You answer my warm greeting
With the roar of your engine,
Wild Goose of the Harvest,
Tractor and combine,
Mechanical bird of the frontier,
Bringing in the grain.

annotations.note: Swan Geese of Harvest — a poetic metaphor serving as messengers bringing news of a bumper crop to distant families, adapting the ancient motif of 'swan geese carrying letters' to the modern reclamation context.

poems.title: Brewing a Life Sweet as Honey
lines: The oasis gleams crystalline,
As the beekeepers ready their hives for the southern journey.
Each autumn they migrate with the bees,
Chasing blossoms across the mountains.
Brewing a life sweet as honey,
These nomads of sweetness,
These followers of flowers,
Who know every valley
By the taste of its nectar.

poems.title: O, the Tinkling Camel Bells
lines: We barely had time for a heart-to-heart talk
Before they set off again;
O, the tinkling camel bells
Of the mobile repair team,
Crossing the desert with spare parts and grease,
A traveling workshop
On the back of a camel.
In the vastness of Junggar,
Even repairs require a journey.

poems.title: Sand Lashes at Anuer's Temples
lines: Sand lashes at Anuer's temples,
Sand lashes at Anuer's skirt,
The ash-brown desert stretches without end.
She walks into the wind
As though the wind were nothing,
This Uyghur girl who chose to stay
When others chose to leave.
Her headscarf streams behind her
Like a flag of quiet courage.

poems.title: I Gently Stroke the Vast Waves of the Desert Sea
lines: I gently stroke the vast waves of the desert sea,
Here, solemnly, I pay my respects to a ruin.
Where is the moon of the Qin Dynasty?
Where are the beacon fires of the Han?
Under my feet lies a history
Of caravans and conquerors,
Of cities that rose and fell
Like waves in this ocean of sand.
I gently stroke the desert
And feel two thousand years of footsteps.

annotations.note: Hanhai ('Vast Sea') — a classical poetic term referring to the boundless northern deserts or Gobi expanses, specifically the immense Gurbantünggüt Desert in this context.

poems.title: Wandering into the Sandstorm in a Distant Foreign Land — The Pioneer's Dream
lines: Swaying poplars,
Galloping snowmelt,
The floating horizon;
In a distant foreign land, walking into the sandstorm,
The pioneer dreams of a city
That does not yet exist,
Of roads that are still camel tracks,
Of gardens that are still desert.
But the dream is so vivid
He can smell the apricot blossoms
And hear the schoolbell ringing
In the city that will rise
From the sand beneath his feet.

annotations.note: Pioneers (tuōhuāngzhě) — referring to the soldiers and youth who became farmers, reclaiming barren desert wastelands for agriculture within the Xinjiang Production and Construction Corps.

poems.title: Bidding Farewell to These Ruins for Now
lines: Bidding farewell to these ruins for now,
Let me put on the wings of imagination.
No rain can be wrung from the clouds here,
So we will dig canals instead.
In the desert, dreamers dig.
Bidding farewell to ruins
Means greeting the future,
Shovel in hand.

poems.title: A Blue Sapphire Set in the Pioneered Oasis
lines: The traveler gazes from his ship of the Gobi,
A blue sapphire set in the pioneered oasis;
Could distant Sayram Lake have tumbled down here?
No — this is a reservoir,
Dug by hand, filled by snowmelt,
A jewel we made ourselves
In the brown setting of the desert.

poems.title: That Is Mother Waving Her Arms
lines: Who painted a stroke of cooking smoke on the desert evening?
The setting sun drifts on the lake where golden serpents glimmer;
The fishing boats should have returned by now —
That is Mother waving her arms
From the shore of the new reservoir,
Calling her children home for dinner,
As mothers have done
Since the first fire was lit.

poems.title: Woven with So Many Threads of Deep Feeling
lines: Night hides the majestic crystal snow-crown of the Tianshan,
And hides the surging snowmelt waves,
And hides the nimble motor-sailboat.
But cannot hide the songs
Woven with so many threads of deep feeling,
Rising from the barracks,
Songs of home and longing,
Songs that travel farther than light
Through the darkness of the frontier.

poems.title: O, What a Vast Stretch of Pale Blue Morning Mist
lines: The moon has slipped from the dusky sky,
The stars have slipped from the dusky sky,
O, what a vast stretch of pale blue morning mist.
The desert at dawn
Is another country entirely,
Cool and gentle and blue,
Before the sun arrives
With its golden hammer.

poems.title: Peering through the Theodolite
lines: Spotting a low-flying bird,
Spotting the green seedlings,
Peering through the theodolite;
The surveyor maps the future
One measurement at a time,
Plotting roads and canals and power lines
Across a landscape that has known
Only wind and sand
For a thousand years.

poems.title: The Little Bird Wearing a Tall Crown of Feathers
lines: Just having flown over this brown wasteland,
Just arriving at this future oasis,
The Tianshan snowmelt grooms your feathers;
Little hoopoe, crested messenger,
You arrived before the trees did,
Before the gardens and the shade,
As though you knew
What we were building
And wanted to be first.

poems.title: Only to Vie for Spring
lines: Where has the green winter wheat hidden?
Where have the silver-grey shelter belts hidden?
The snowstorm's waves have swallowed the oasis we developed,
But only until spring —
Only until spring,
When the green will burst from the white
Like a shout of triumph,
And the shelter belts will reappear
And the wheat will stand again,
Only to vie for spring.

poems.title: Chasing along the Trail of the Wind
lines: The Gurbantünggüt is as solemn as the sea,
The cloudless sky is clear azurite blue;
A wisp of wind cannot escape the watchful eye,
For these are sand-fixation scouts,
Chasing along the trail of the wind,
Planting grass and tamarisk
In the wind's own footprints,
Trapping the dunes in green nets
Of their own devising.

poems.title: 'Point A — Point A...'
lines: Tawny dunes meeting tawny sky,
A lone boat sailing the surging desert sea;
The melody of a marching song drifts on the air,
And the surveyor calls out his coordinates
'Point A — Point A...'
Marking the spot
Where a school will stand,
Or a hospital,
Or a park with trees and children's laughter,
In twenty years —
In fifty —
'Point A — Point A...'

poems.title: The Blooming Snowflakes Have Already Withered
lines: The blooming snowflakes have already withered,
Silently withered from the low-hanging sky;
Falling on the desert's solid reservoir — the Tianshan's icy peaks,
To be stored and saved
For the long, hot summer,
When the canals will drink the glaciers
And carry their cold sweetness
Down to the fields,
Down to the orchards,
Down to the green dream
That winter is quietly building.

chapters.title: Volume II: The General and the City

poems.title: He Died before Dawn
lines: He died before dawn,
The general who had built the city,
Who had turned soldiers into masons and farmers,
Who said: 'We will not leave this place.'
His last breath floated upward
Like the smoke of a campfire going out,
And the city he built
Went on without him,
Its streets filling with morning light.

annotations.note: Before Dawn — a poem honoring martyrs who died right before the founding of the new state. 'Dawn' metaphorically represents the peaceful liberation of Xinjiang in September 1949.

poems.title: She Too Became a Dark-Gold Wave of Signal
lines: Her radio signal traveled outward from the small room,
Through the Tianshan night,
Through the static of distance —
She too became a dark-gold wave of signal,
This woman of the frontier,
Who transmitted weather readings
And troop positions
And love letters
Across the crackle and hiss of the desert airwaves.

poems.title: Prometheus Lit the Heavy Curtain of Night
lines: Prometheus lit the heavy curtain of night —
Or was it the electrician from Shandong
Who strung the first power line
Across the Gurbantünggüt,
Bringing electric light
To a place that had known
Only candle, campfire, and star?
Prometheus lit the heavy curtain of night,
And the desert blinked
In astonishment.

annotations.note: Prometheus — the Titan from Greek mythology who stole fire for humanity. The poet uses this metaphor to praise the revolutionary pioneers who brought light and hope to the darkness of Xinjiang.

poems.title: One Must Pass through the Night to Reach the Shore of Dawn
lines: One must pass through the night to reach the shore of dawn —
This was the motto
Of every pioneer on the frontier,
Every soldier turned farmer,
Every woman who followed her regiment
Into the sand.
The night was long,
The cold was deep,
But dawn came, as it always comes,
With the rooster's cry and the kettle's steam,
And the first light on the Tianshan snow.

poems.title: Arriving in Haste from Afar
lines: Arriving in haste from afar,
Boots still dusty from the Hexi Corridor,
The new volunteers step off the truck
Into the blinding light of Junggar.
They have come from Sichuan, from Hunan,
From the green hills of Zhejiang,
To this brown, flat, enormous nowhere
That will become their home.
Arriving in haste from afar
With nothing but youth
And the government's promise
Of land.

poems.title: An Emerald River Flows
lines: An emerald river flows
Through the heart of the new city,
A canal dug by ten thousand hands
From the Tianshan glacier to the cotton fields.
Where once only camels drank,
Now children swim and splash,
And willows line the banks
Of this manufactured miracle,
This emerald river
That flows because someone
Decided it would.

poems.title: That Dreamland of Life Longed for in Visions
lines: That dreamland of life longed for in visions
Is no longer a vision:
The cotton fields stretch to the horizon,
The poplars stand in soldier-straight rows,
The school bell rings at eight.
This was the dream —
Not of conquest,
But of cultivation,
Not of empire,
But of a life worth living
On the edge of the world.

poems.title: Deep Affection Has Melted into the Growth Rings of History
lines: Deep affection has melted into the growth rings of history,
Each ring a year of toil,
A year of planting and harvesting,
A year farther from the war,
A year closer to ordinary life.
Count the rings of the poplar trees
That line the streets of the new city:
Each ring is a generation,
Each generation a prayer answered.

poems.title: Those Emerald Battlements
lines: Those emerald battlements —
Not of stone, but of poplar and willow —
Stand guard around the city
The soldiers built.
No cannon-fire here,
Only the rustle of leaves,
Only the sound of children
Playing in the shade
Of walls that are trees,
Of fortresses that are forests.

annotations.note: Battlements — defensive walls on a fortress. Here, the 'emerald battlements' symbolically refer to the green forest belts planted by the soldiers to protect the newly built desert oasis city.

poems.title: Like a Beautiful Phoenix Reborn from Fire
lines: Like a beautiful phoenix reborn from fire,
The city rose from the desert,
Shihezi, Stone River —
Named for the stones in the riverbed,
Built by men who knew
What it means to start from nothing.
Like a phoenix,
It should not exist,
And yet it does,
Green and shining,
In the brown heart of Junggar.

poems.title: The Green Arrow Speeds Forward
lines: The green arrow speeds forward —
A new highway cutting through the Gobi,
Connecting city to city,
Dream to dream.
Where camel bells once marked the pace,
Now engines hum,
And the green arrow
Of progress speeds forward,
From Ürümqi to Shihezi to the border,
Faster than the wind,
But not so ancient.

poems.title: That Is Not the Tawny Morning Mist
lines: That is not the tawny morning mist —
It is the dust of construction,
The golden cloud raised by a thousand shovels,
As another building rises
From the desert floor.
That is not mist — it is the breath
Of a city being born.

poems.title: Plowing Out a Full, Round Red Sun
lines: At the city's edge, the tractor turns,
Plowing out a full, round red sun
From the dark earth,
As though the earth itself
Were giving birth to light.
This is the frontier at dawn,
Where every furrow is a line of poetry,
Where every harvest is a verse,
And the red sun rises
From the turning blade.

chapters.title: Volume III: A Poet in the Gurbantünggüt

poems.title: The Brook Splashes with Brown Waves
lines: The brook splashes with brown waves,
Carrying the color of the desert's skin
Down through the poplar-lined gully,
Past the stones polished by centuries of flow.
A poet walks beside this brook,
His notebook open to the wind,
Catching words as they fly past
Like seedpods from the tamarisk.
What language does the desert speak?
The poet listens,
Bends to the water,
And writes.

annotations.note: Gurbantünggüt — China's second largest desert, located in the center of the Junggar Basin in northern Xinjiang, featuring vast expanses of fixed and semi-fixed sand dunes.

poems.title: A Green Cloud Drifts from the Distance
lines: A green cloud drifts from the distance —
Not a cloud at all, but a line of poplars
Planted by the pioneers,
Now tall enough to change the sky.
In the Gurbantünggüt,
Where nothing grew,
Now a green cloud drifts,
A forest floating in the desert,
An impossible garden
Rooted in faith.
A green cloud drifts from the distance,
And the poet, seeing it,
Weeps.

poems.title: The Nocturne of Silk Road Camel Bells Still Sways...
lines: At the heart of the Gurbantünggüt,
Where the ancient northern route once passed,
The nocturne of Silk Road camel bells still sways...
If you press your ear to the sand at midnight,
You can hear them —
The ghost-bells of a thousand caravans,
Ringing through the centuries,
Faint as starlight,
Persistent as wind.
The poet here is never alone:
He walks with ghosts of merchants,
With monks and soldiers,
With every soul who ever crossed
This vast and singing desert.

annotations.note: Silk Road — the ancient network of trade routes connecting China to Central and West Asia, and Europe, named for the Chinese silk that was heavily traded westward.

poems.title: White Peonies
lines: White peonies —
In the Gurbantünggüt?
Impossible, says the botanist.
And yet here they are,
Blooming in the lee of a dune,
Snow-white and improbable,
Like the cities the pioneers built.
White peonies,
You are the proof
That even the desert
Has a taste for beauty.

poems.title: In the Cold Night, Lighting This Campfire
lines: In the cold night, lighting this campfire,
The poet watches the flames lean in the wind,
Sparks spiraling toward the Milky Way,
Each spark a verse,
Each verse a spark.
This is the oldest poem —
Fire in the dark,
A human hand extending warmth
Toward the indifferent stars.
In the cold night, lighting this campfire,
The poet thinks:
I am not the first here,
And I will not be the last,
But tonight this flame is mine,
And this desert,
And these stars.

chapters.title: Volume IV: For Love, Baghdad Is Not Too Far

poems.title: O, Nightingale
lines: O, nightingale,
Why do you sing in the red willow thickets
When the moon has already set?
Is your song for the darkness
Or for the girl who listens
From her window in the barracks?
O, nightingale,
The frontier has enough silence;
Thank you for breaking it
With your small, wild voice.

annotations.note: Nightingale — a songbird frequently found in Xinjiang's red willow thickets. In Li Yu's poetry, it is an enduring symbol of love, lyricism, and melancholy, central to the Junggar imagery.

poems.title: The Gentle, Unceasing Raindrops
lines: The gentle, unceasing raindrops
Fall on the tin roof of the dugout,
A rhythm soft as a lullaby,
Soft as her voice when she said:
'I will wait for you.'
Rain in the desert is a miracle,
As rare as love,
And as welcome.

poems.title: That Is a Crescent Moon behind the Dune
lines: That is a crescent moon behind the dune,
Rising like a silver eyelash
Above the sand's brown brow.
Under this crescent moon
I write her name in the sand,
Knowing the wind will erase it by morning,
Knowing I will write it again tomorrow night.

poems.title: She Has Already Filled the Jug with Water
lines: She has already filled the jug with water
From the irrigation canal,
The evening light catching the droplets
On her brown arms.
In the Junggar dusk,
Everything turns to gold:
The water, the wheat, her hair,
The dust on her sandals,
The smile she gives me
Without turning around.

poems.title: Was That Her Last Smile at Me?
lines: Was that her last smile at me?
The truck pulled away,
And through the dust I thought I saw her wave —
Or was it the wind in the red willows?
She went back to Sichuan,
To the green hills and the rice paddies,
Away from the sand and the wind,
Away from me.
Was that her last smile at me?
I have been asking the desert ever since,
And the desert does not answer.

poems.title: O, the Nightingale in the Red Willows
lines: O, the nightingale in the red willows
Sings the song I cannot sing,
The song of a man who stayed
When the woman he loved left.
The nightingale does not know loneliness,
But it sings as though it does,
And listening, I almost believe
That beauty and sadness
Are the same thing.

poems.title: To Weave the Spring Colors of the Frontier
lines: 'To weave the spring colors of the frontier' —
She said that was why she came,
A girl from the silk factories of Suzhou,
Who traded her loom for a shovel.
She plants flowers along the canal bank,
Marigolds and hollyhocks,
Bright against the brown earth,
Weaving a different kind of silk:
The colors of spring
On the frontier's rough cloth.

poems.title: A Green Melody Flows
lines: A green melody flows
Through the newly planted shelter belt,
The wind playing the poplars like a flute,
Each tree a note in the score.
I listen with my eyes closed
And hear the future:
A city's worth of shade,
A province's worth of calm,
All flowing from this green melody
That began as a seedling
In a soldier's hand.

poems.title: O, Please Don't Leave Me
lines: O, please don't leave me,
Not now, not when the cotton is about to bloom,
Not when the reservoir is nearly full,
Not when we've almost built the life
We've been talking about since Lanzhou.
Stay until the poplars are taller than us,
Stay until the road is paved,
Stay until the stars feel less like strangers
And more like neighbors.
O, please don't leave me.
The desert is wide enough for two.

poems.title: That Is a Wheel Sent Flying by a Sharp Dune
lines: That is a wheel sent flying by a sharp dune,
Bouncing across the sand
Like a small, ridiculous moon.
The truck groaned and tilted,
And the supplies scattered:
Cans of paint, rolls of wire,
And love letters
From the mail bag,
Tumbling in the sand
Beneath the indifferent sky.

poems.title: O, Those Dark-Blue Snow Waves
lines: O, those dark-blue snow waves
Crashing down from the Tianshan at dusk,
Carrying the cold breath of the glacier
Into the warm valley below.
Standing in the snow waves' mist,
I think of you,
Your dark-blue cotton dress,
Your hair loose in the wind,
Your laughter like the water —
Cold and bright and rushing.

annotations.note: Snow Waves — referring to the visual of snowmelt streams plunging from the Tianshan Mountains into the desert margins. It serves as a striking metaphor for vitality and impassioned emotion.

poems.title: She Walked Away, Smiling Gracefully
lines: She walked away, smiling gracefully,
Down the poplar-lined road,
Her suitcase in one hand,
Her future in the other.
She did not look back —
Or if she did,
The poplars hid her face.
She walked away smiling,
As though leaving were easy,
As though the desert
Had not already
Written its name
On her heart.

poems.title: I Am Going to Where She Lives
lines: I am going to where she lives,
Past the dunes and the dry riverbeds,
Past the red willow stands,
Past the survey markers
And the half-built road.
I am going to where she lives,
In the new settlement south of the ridge,
Where she teaches twenty children
In a schoolroom made of mud brick.
I am going to where she lives.
The desert is only fifty kilometers.
For love, Baghdad is not too far.

poems.title: I Gently Stroke This Blood-Red Gobi Stone
lines: I gently stroke this blood-red Gobi stone,
Polished by a million years of wind,
Small as a heart,
Warm from the afternoon sun.
I will give it to her
Instead of a ring.
On the frontier
We do not have jewelers,
But we have the desert,
And the desert makes
Its own gems.

poems.title: Walking in Silence
lines: Walking in silence
Through the Gurbantünggüt at dusk,
The sand still warm beneath my feet,
The sky turning colors
That have no name in any language.
Walking in silence
Because some feelings
Are too large for words,
Too wide for the mouth,
And must be carried
In the chest,
In the stride,
In the silence itself.

poems.title: O, in My Heart There Is a Moon That Never Sets
lines: O, in my heart there is a moon that never sets,
A moon that shines when the sky is overcast,
A moon that glows in the sandstorm,
A moon that follows me
From dugout to field to canal bank.
I do not need
The moon above the Tianshan,
For I carry my own moon,
Bright with the memory of her face,
Full every night,
Never waning.

poems.title: The Brook Flows through the Dark-Blue Night
lines: The brook flows through the dark-blue night,
A silver thread in the darkness,
Carrying the sound of the glacier
Down to the sleeping fields.
I sit beside it and remember:
How she dipped her feet in this water,
How she laughed when the cold shocked her,
How she said: 'Even the water here
Tastes like adventure.'
The brook flows on
Through the dark-blue night,
Carrying her laughter with it
Eastward, always eastward.

poems.title: I Can Do without the Moon in the Sky
lines: I can do without the moon in the sky,
I can do without the rain from the clouds,
I can do without the letters from home.
But I cannot do without
The thought of you,
Standing in the doorway
Of the mud-brick schoolhouse,
The chalk still on your fingers,
Teaching twenty children
How to write the word 'spring'
In a land of perpetual winter.

poems.title: Those Deep Echoes from the Nights of First Love
lines: Those deep echoes from the nights of first love
Still reverberate in the desert air,
In the spaces between the dunes,
In the gaps between the stars.
We were young then,
As young as the saplings we planted,
As green as the wheat we watered,
As foolish as the nightingale
Who sings to an empty desert.
Those deep echoes
Have never stopped.

poems.title: The Bird That Heralds the Dawn
lines: The bird that heralds the dawn
Sits on the highest branch
Of the tallest poplar in the shelter belt,
And sings.
Not a nightingale this time,
But a lark,
Bright-voiced and common,
The bird of ordinary mornings,
Of coffee and bread and work.
The bird that heralds the dawn
Sings not of love
But of the beautiful routine
Of days that follow days
On the frontier.

poems.title: Though It Is the Same Kind of Sadness
lines: Though it is the same kind of sadness
As the night the train pulled away,
It is a gentler sadness now,
Worn smooth by years,
Like a Gobi stone in the hand.
I have learned to live with it,
This sadness that is also gratitude,
This longing that is also memory,
This absence that is also presence,
Here, in the desert,
Where everything is far away
And nothing is forgotten.

poems.title: Like a Mournful Nightingale Flying toward Me
lines: Like a mournful nightingale flying toward me,
The memory of her returns
On every desert evening,
On every crescent moon.
I open my arms to catch it,
This dark bird of memory,
And hold it to my chest,
And listen to its song:
For love, Baghdad is not too far.
For love, no distance is too great.
For love, the desert is not a desert
But a garden waiting for rain.
Like a mournful nightingale flying toward me,
She returns and returns,
And I, who stayed,
Am the richer for it.
